content.full_text:
Re:
Fnrn PolrrrcAt- PRACTICES CoMMISSIoN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento. CA 95811-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
November 16,2007
Roy A. Hanley
Interim Assistant City Attorney
City of Atascadero
8930 Morro Road
Atascadero, CA93422
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-173
Dear Mr. Hanley:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Atascadero
Parks and Recreation Commissioner Jonalee Istenes regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTON
May Commissioner Istenes who has economic interests in three real properties
within 500 feet of the Atascadero Lake Parl< make, participate in making, or influence
governmental decisions regarding specific projects within the park if the boundaries of
the projects are over 500 feet from the boundaries of the three properties?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, urrless otherw'ise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-07-173
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in real property are directly involved in
the governmental decisions regarding the specific projects within the park. The financial
effect of these decisions is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Commissioner Istenes
may not make, participate in making, or influence these decisions unless she can prove
that the presumption of materiality has been rebutted and determine that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on her other economic interests.
FACTS
Jonalee Istenes is a recently appointed member of the City of Atascadero Parks
and Recreation Commission (the "APRC"). Commissioner Istenes owns three properties
near the Atascadero Lake Park including her own home and two rental properties. All
three of these properties are within 500 feet of the boundaries of the park.
The Atascadero Lake Park is the City of Atascadero's largest recreational facility
According to the map you have provided, the park is approximately 50 acres. Roughly
half of the 50 total acres consists of the lake itself. The amenities located at the park
include a 10,000 square foot pavilion used for banquets, receptions, and conferences; the
County of San Luis Obispo's only zoo; baseball diamonds; picnic areas; and other
general recreational facilities.
It is anticipated that five specific issues or projects will be presented in the near
future for the APRC's consideration. These projects include the following:
l. General rules for paddleboats - The APRC may consider new or revised rules
regarding the use of paddleboats on the lake. The lake currently has a concession for
renting paddleboats. While the concession itself is more than 500 feet from the real
property owned by Commissioner Istenes, the boundaries of the lake are within 500 feet
of the commissioner's property.
2. Frontal improvemenrs - Currently the city's master plan calls for frontal
improvements along Morro Road to the zoo and park. The construction sites and the zoo
are more than 500 feet from the real property owned by Commissioner Istenes.
3. Botanical gardens - The APRC may consider the placement of botanical
gardens within the park. The boundaries of the botanical gardens may be more than 500
feet from the real property owned by Commissioner Istenes.
4. Veterans' memorial - The city council has already approved a veterans'
memorial in the park. The area planned for the memorial, while not finalized, is more
than 500 feet from the real property owned by Commissioner Istenes.


File No. A-07-173
Page No. 3
5. Exercise stcttions - A city program is installing exercise stations along a trail
that encircles the lake. While portions of the trail are within 500 feet of the real property
owned by Commissioner Istenes, some exercise stations that will need to be considered
will be located along portions of the trail more than 500 feet from the real property
owned by the commissioner.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the govemmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)
Step One: Is Commissioner Istenes a "public official?"
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency ...."
(Section 82048.) As a member of the APRC, Commissioner Istenes is a public official
within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Is Commissioner Istenes making, participating in making, or influencing
a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreementonbehalf of hisorheragency. (Regulation 15702.1.) Apublicofficial
"participates in a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) Commissioner Istenes is making, participating in making, or
influencing a govemmental decision when acting in her role as a commissioner on the
APRC.


a
a
File No. A-07-173
Page No.4
Step Three: What are Commissioner Istenes' "economic interests?"
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (Section 87 103(d); Regulation 18703. 1(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within l2 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); Regulation
18703.4);
a
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5).
Real Property:
You have stated that Commissioner Istenes owns three properties within 500 feet
of the Atascadero Lake Park. From all indications it appears that her investment in each
of the three properties is $2,000 or more. Accordingly, Commissioner Istenes has an
economic interest in each of the three properties. (Section 87103(b).)
Rental Business
You have indicated that two of the three properties owned by Commissioner
Istenes are rental properties. Presumably, Commissioner lstenes has an investment of
$2,000 or more in her rental business; is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
manager of the rental business; and will receive income of $500 or more in the
12-months prior to the decision from the rental business. Therefore, Commissioner
Istenes has an economic interest in her rental business as both a business entity and as a
source of income. (Section 87103 (a), (c), and (d).)


File No. A-07-173
Page No. 5
Tenants:
Providing that Commissioner Istenes receives income of $500 or more in the
l2-months prior to the decision from any particular tenant of the rental unit, she will also
have an economic interest in the tenant as a source of income. (Section 87103(c).)
Personal Finances
Commissioner Istenes also has an economic interest in her personal finances and
those of her immediate family. A govemmental decision will have an effect on this
economic interest if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income, assets or
liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section
87 103; Regulation I 8703.5.)2
Step Four: Is Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in her three real properties
directly or indirectly involved in the decisions?
In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, the official must first determine
if his or her economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulation 1 8704(a).)
The Act states that real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a govemmental decision if any part of the real property is
located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property
that is the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation I87O4.2(aX 1).)
Generally speaking, the decisions coming before the city council relate to the
Atascadero Lake Park, which is within 500 feet of Commissioner Istenes' three
properties. However, you have presented the following facts:
l. Commissioner Istenes' three properties are more than 500 feet from the
concession renting paddleboats for use on the lake.
2. Commissioner Istenes' three properties are more than 500 feet from the
construction sites for the frontal improvements along Morro Road to the park and zoo
2 You have not provided us with sufficient facts to determine whether Commissioner Istenes has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in any one decision because of her economic interests in her rental
business, tenants ofher rental business, or her personal finances. Moreover, in light ofour conclusions in
terms of Commissioner Istenes'economic interests in her three properties, it is unnecessary for us to further
consider her other economic interests. Accordingly. we conclude our analysis of Commissioner Istenes'
economic interests in her rental business, tenants or her rental business, and her personal finances at this
point. If you later determine that one of these economic interests may be impacted by a governmental
decision, it is advisable that you seek further assistance providing all relevant facts.


File No. A-07-173
Page No. 6
3. Commissioner Istenes' three properties may be more than 500 feet from the
borders of the proposed botanical gardens depending on the selected location for the
gardens within the park.
4. Commissioner [stenes' three properties are more than 500 feet from the
proposed location for the veterans' memorial.
5. Commissioner Istenes' three properties are more than 500 feet from some of
the exercise stations located along a trail encircling the lake.
In light of these facts, the pertinent question is whether the Commission will use
the distance between Commissioner lstenes' three properties and the boundary of the
Atascadero Lake Park or the distance between Commissioner Istenes' three properties
and the more specific projects located within the park to determine whether the
commissioner's properties are directly involved in the governmental decisions.
The plain language of the Regulation L8704.2(a)(l) requires that the distance be
measured from the boundaries of the property that is the subject of the governmental
decision. However, in certain circumstances, where the governmental decision affects a
clearly defined, specific, and isolated site, such as a particular building on a large tract of
land, "the Commission has interpreted this regulation to allow the distance to be
measured from that clearly defined and specifically affected portion." (McLaughlin
Advice Letter, No. A-05-061.)'
Turning to the decisions faced by the APRC, the rules regarding the use of
paddleboats will affect the use of the paddleboats on the entire lake. Moreover, decisions
regarding the amenities offered in the park such as paddleboats, the frontal
improvements, botanical gardens, veterans' memorial, and exercise stations will affect
the overall usage of the park.
In terms of a govemmental decision regarding the amenities offered in a large
park, we have previously recognized that a public official is not necessarily disqualified
from the decision if the effects of the decision will be localized to a portion of the park
beyond 500 feet from the official's economic interest in real property. (Barker Advice
Letter, No. 4-03-022.) However, unlike the Barker letter that addressed a decision
t For example, in the Krauel Advice Letter, No. I-92- I 18, the public official owned property
within 300 feet of city-owned land. The land consisted of the city hall, a local television studio, a public
library, and a parking lot. The potentially disqualifying decision concerned the public library. The distance
from the public official's property to the library site was greater than 300 feet but within 2,500 (the
standards in the regulation at that time). The Commission advised that the greater distance was the proper
measure for that decision as long as the decision was limited to the library site. If the decision concerned
all the city-owned land, the shorter distance was to be used. For other examples of the application of this
test see the Bakker Advice Letter, No. A-06-070 and the Barker Advice Letters, No. I-02-050 and A-03-
022.


File No. A-O7-173
Page No. 7
regarding trails within a park encompassing3,6TO acres, the Atascadero Lake Park
consists of just 50 total acres. Considering the size of the Atascadero Lake Park it is
unlikely that the effects of the decisions regarding any of the five specific projects will be
localized to the site of the project and not the remainder of the park. Accordingly,
Commissioner Istenes' interests in the three properties are considered directly involved in
the governmental decisions.
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Commissioner Istenes' economic interests?
Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved and determined that each
interests in real property is directly involved in the decisions at issue, it is necessary to
identify the materiality standard appropriate to Commissioner lstenes' economic interests
in real property.
Any financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(aX1).)
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property. (lbid.)
However, the size of the financial effect does not matter. If there is any financial effect at
all, even "one-penny," that effect is presumed to be "material."
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (ln
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
However, you have indicated a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in real property. As previously addressed, the
financial effect on Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in real property is presumed


File No. A-07-173
Page No. 8
to be material because the properties are within 500 feet of the property subject to the
governmental decision. Accordingly, Commissioner lstenes may not make, participate in
making, or influence the APRC's decisions regarding the five specific projects within the
Atascadero Lake Park unless she can rebut the presumption of materiality and determine
that there will be /ro reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on her other
economic interests such as her rental business or tenants of her rental properties.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
Commissioner Istenes' circumstances, so we will not analyze them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGL:jgl

Materiality
Having identified the economic interests involved and determined that each

identify the materiality standard appropriate to Commissioner lstenes' economic interests
in real property.
Any financial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(aX1).)
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property. (lbid.)
However, the size of the financial effect does not matter. If there is any financial effect at
all, even "one-penny," that effect is presumed to be "material."
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra,
at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (ln
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
However, you have indicated a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in real property. As previously addressed, the
financial effect on Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in real property is presumed
to be material because the properties are within 500 feet of the property subject to the
governmental decision. Accordingly, Commissioner lstenes may not make, participate in
making, or influence the APRC's decisions regarding the five specific projects within the
Atascadero Lake Park unless she can rebut the presumption of materiality and determine
that there will be /ro reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on her other
economic interests such as her rental business or tenants of her rental properties.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to
Commissioner Istenes' circumstances, so we will not analyze them further.

the projects are over 500 feet from the boundaries of the three properties?
'Page No. 2

CONCLUSION: Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in real property are directly involved in
the governmental decisions regarding the specific projects within the park. The financial
effect of these decisions is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Commissioner Istenes
may not make, participate in making, or influence these decisions unless she can prove
that the presumption of materiality has been rebutted and determine that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on her other economic interests.

Hanley Interim Assistant City Attorney City of Atascadero 8930 Morro Road Atascadero, CA93422 Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-07-173 Dear Mr. Hanley: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Atascadero Parks and Recreation Commissioner Jonalee Istenes regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.) Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTTON May Commissioner Istenes who has economic interests in three real properties within 500 feet of the Atascadero Lake Parl< make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions regarding specific projects within the park if the boundaries of the projects are over 500 feet from the boundaries of the three properties? ' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, urrless otherw'ise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-07-173 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Commissioner Istenes' economic interests in real property are directly involved in the governmental decisions regarding the specific projects within the park. The financial effect of these decisions is presumed to be material. Accordingly, Commissioner Istenes may not make, participate in making, or influence these decisions unless she can prove that the presumption of materiality has been rebutted and determine that there will be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effects on her other economic interests. FACTS Jonalee Istenes is a recently appointed member of the City of Atascadero Parks and Recreation Commission (the "APRC"). Commissioner Istenes owns three properties near the Atascadero Lake Park including her own home and two rental properties. All three of these properties are within 500 feet of the boundaries of the park. The Atascadero Lake Park is the City of Atascadero's largest recreational facility According to the map you have provided, the park is approximately 50 acres. Roughly half of the 50 total acres consists of the lake itself. The amenities located at the park include a 10,000 square foot pavilion used for banquets, receptions, and conferences; the County of San Luis Obispo's
